# Project Charta
## Context and Scope
Business travel is common in many companies. Employees travel for meetings, conferences, or to work with colleagues in other locations. However, business travel, especially flights, also produces CO₂ emissions, which has an impact on the environment.

In this project, we will analyse and visualize a dataset about business travel. The dataset includes information about transport mode, travel distance, travel purpose, and estimated CO₂ emissions.
The dataset covers business trips from 2017 to early 2026 and represents travel activities within one company with several business units.

The goal of this project is to create an interactive dashboard that helps users understand:

- how travel behaviour changes over time
- why people travel (travel purpose)
- how transport choices affect CO₂ emissions

The dashboard should help answer questions such as:

- How have travel emissions changed over time?
- Which transport modes produce the most CO₂ emissions?
- Which travel purposes (e.g. meetings, conferences) occur most often and contribute most to CO₂ emissions?
- How do different travel options compare in terms of CO₂ emissions and travel duration?

The dashboard should also support simple decisions by allowing users to compare transport modes and understand how much CO₂ different trips produce.
In addition, the dashboard helps users understand which travel purposes cause high CO₂ emissions. This can support reflection on whether certain trips are necessary or if alternative solutions, such as online meetings, could be considered.

The visualization product will be available on a website.

## Project Objectives and Success Criteria
From a stakeholder perspective, the project aims to:

- Provide a clear overview of business travel and CO₂ emissions
- Show how emissions develop over time
- Identify which travel purposes generate the most emissions
- Compare transport modes and their environmental impact
- Support users in understanding and comparing travel options

The final result will be an interactive dashboard that allows users to explore the dataset


Qualitative success criteria:

- The dashboard allows users to quickly understand travel emissions and patterns without needing manual data preparation
- Users can identify trends over time and detect changes in travel behaviour (e.g. increase or decrease of emissions).
- Users can clearly see which travel purposes cause the most CO₂ emissions and reflect on whether these trips are necessary.
- The dashboard supports users in communicating insights (e.g. for reports or presentations) using clear and simple visualizations.

Quantitative success criteria:

- Users are able to identify key trends (e.g. increase or decrease in emissions) within 30–60 seconds.
- Users can filter the data by at least two attributes (e.g., year, transport mode).
- Test users can identify at least two insights about travel patterns within one minute of interacting with the dashboard.


*Out of Scope:* The project will not include recommendations for specific travel options or policies. 

## Stakeholder Analysis
List the people involved in and affected by the project. Describe their goals and relationships with each other. Visualisation in the form of a stakeholder map can provide a quick overview.
?????? Séverine Stadler

| Stakeholder | Project Role | Goals and Interests | Power | Interest |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Laura Smith (Sustainability Manager / SM)** | Primary User | Needs accurate data to monitor corporate CO₂ targets, generate annual reports, and justify new sustainable travel policies. | High | High |
| **Mark Johnson (Business Unit Manager / BUM)** | Key User | Wants to balance financial travel budgets with allocated CO₂ limits while maintaining team productivity. | High | High |
| **Management** | Project Sponsors | Focused on overarching corporate goals, achieving ESG mandates, and reducing overall business costs. | High | Medium |
| **Employees**| Affected Group | Directly impacted by travel policies derived from the dashboard. Interested in transparency and efficient routes. | Low | High |
| **IT / Web-Administration** | Support and Infrastructure | Responsible for the technical integration of the dashboard into the company’s website. Focused on security and stability. | High | Low |
| **Project Team** | Creators and Developers | Goal is to successfully clean the data, build an intuitive dashboard, and meet the project requirements. | Medium | High |
| **Travel Agency** | Data Provider (External) | Processes bookings and provides raw data. No direct interest in the internal visualization. | Low | Low |

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {"theme": "base", "themeVariables": {"quadrant1Fill": "#b1facc", "quadrant2Fill": "#bbdcff", "quadrant3Fill": "#ffb9b9" , "quadrant4Fill": "#ffffb7"}}}%%
quadrantChart
    title Stakeholder Matrix
    x-axis "Low interest" --> "High interest"
    y-axis "Low Power" --> "High Power"

    "SM": [0.95, 0.85]
    "Management": [0.52, 0.90]
    "BUM": [0.75, 0.80]
    "Project Team": [0.75, 0.50]
    "IT / Web-Admin": [0.25, 0.90]
    "Employee": [0.80, 0.10]
    "Travel Agency": [0.10, 0.10]

```

## User Analysis
Analyse the target audience of the visualization product and create at least 2 personas with **relevant attributes** such as domain expertise, data literacy, technical environment (devices, screen sizes), frequency of use etc. Amend each persona with the dimensions of the customer profile side in the Value Proposition Canvas @Osterwalder2014:

* **User tasks** (jobs): What tasks are users trying to accomplish? Consider functional jobs (e.g. analysing data, making decisions), social jobs (e.g. reporting to stakeholders, collaborating with colleagues) and emotional jobs (e.g. feeling confident in their decisions).
* **Pains**: What obstacles, risks or undesired outcomes do users currently face? What makes their current approach frustrating or inefficient?
* **Gains**: What outcomes and benefits do users expect or desire? What would exceed their expectations?

### **Persona 1: Sustainability Manager**
![](../charta-pictures/laura_smith.png)

- **Name**: Laura Smith
- **Age**: 32
- **Role**: Sustainability Manager
- **Nationality**: British
- **Degree**: Master’s in Environmental Science
- **Domain Expertise**: High (expert in sustainability and environmental impact)
- **Data Literacy**: Medium (comfortable with data but not an expert)
- **Technical Environment**: Desktop computer with a large monitor for analysis, but manages everything via cloud tools on her tablet and phone while agile working.
- **Frequency of Use**: Daily (for monitoring and reporting purposes)

Laura lifes in a minimalistic, shared apartment in Zurich-West, filled with upcycled furniture and numerous indoor plants. She commutes daily by bicycle or train, valuing the physical activity and the emission consequences travel. Sundays are spent hiking in the Alps, combining her love for nature with physical challenge. She speaks fluent English and professional German. Curreently she is trying to learn some basic Swiss German to better connect with her local colleagues and stakeholders.

She joined the company 1.5 years ago as a Senior Sustainability Analyst and was promoted to Manager 4 months ago due to her ambition and early successes in automating initial reporting workflows. She expects tools to work instantly. A clunky, buggy software experience causes her immediate frustration. Her philosophy is: "Let the data flow, then let the analysis begin." 

As a digital native, Laura treats data as a product. She doesn't just analyze it, she expects it to be auditable, scalable, and beautifully visual. She is passionate about using data to drive real change in corporate sustainability and is always looking for ways to make complex information more accessible and actionable for her colleagues and senior leadership. 

Her main goal is to reduce the company's carbon footprint from business travel and to communicate the company's sustainability efforts effectively to stakeholders. She is also focused on identifying trends in travel behaviour and emissions to inform future strategies and policies. Laura is under pressure to deliver quick results due to new regulations and corporate commitments, but she is determined to use data-driven insights to make a meaningful impact on the company's sustainability performance.

**User Tasks:**

- Monitor and report on the company's CO₂ emissions from business travel
- Identify trends and patterns in travel behaviour and emissions
- prepare reports and presentations for senior management and stakeholders
- Convince skeptical senior leadership of sustainable investments using hard financial and environmental data.

**Pains:**

- **Data Mess:** Spends 70% of her time chasing incomplete Excel sheets from different departments instead of analyzing data.
- **Resistance:** Faced with "We’ve always done it this way" from older managers when she proposes train travel over short-haul flights.
- **Pressure:** The board expects instant, ESG results due to new regulations.
- **Difficulty:** accessing and analyzing travel data from multiple sources
- **Time-consuming:** process to create reports and visualizations manually

**Gains:**

- **A "Single Source of Truth":** An interactive dashboard where all travel data flows together automatically and cleanly.
- **Automated Reporting:** Being able to generate the annual travel emission report with three clicks, saving days of manual work.
- **Visual Power:** Professional, undeniable visualizations that make the argument for a "Train First" policy obvious to anyone.
- **Ability to quickly identify and analyze** travel patterns and emissions
- **Improved decision-making** capabilities based on data-driven insights
- **Enhanced communication** of sustainability efforts to stakeholders through clear visualizations

#### **Use Case Scenario: Sustainability Manager**

**Context:**

 It is the end of the year, and Laura needs to prepare the annual sustainability report for the executive board. She has to present the overall trends in business travel emissions from the past few years and propose new emission reduction strategies for the upcoming year.

**The Action:**

 Instead of spending days manually collecting Excel sheets from different departments and building complex pivot tables, Laura opens the Business Travel Dashboard on her desktop monitor. She navigates to the "Time Series & Overview" tab to analyze the company's total carbon footprint over the last few years.

**The Insight (How the dashboard helps):**

- **Instant Trend Identification:** The dashboard shows her a clear line chart of CO₂ emissions over time. She filters the data by transport mode and quickly identifies a trend: while flight emissions have slowly decreased, emissions from rental cars have spiked significantly in the last two years.
- **Effortless Reporting:** She uses the dashboard's filtering to dive deeper and sees that these rental car emissions are mostly coming from trips under 300 km, which could easily be replaced by train travel.

**The Outcome:**

Because the visualizations are already clean and professional, Laura simply exports the charts and copies them directly into her presentation for the board. The dashboard saved her hours of frustrating manual data aggregation. In the board meeting, she confidently uses the insights to successfully argue for a new company-wide policy that mandates train travel for all distances under 300 km.


### **Persona 2: Business Unit Manager**
- **Name**: Mark Johnson
- **Role**: Business Unit Manager
- **Domain Expertise**: High in business operations; Medium in sustainability (growing awareness due to new corporate ESG mandates).
- **Data Literacy**: Medium (comfortable with business KPIs and budget dashboards, but needs pre-processed visual data for quick insights).
- **Technical Environment**: Desktop/Laptop with standard office software, frequent use of mobile devices while on the go.
- **Frequency of Use**: Monthly (for reporting) and ad-hoc (when approving team travel requests).

**User Tasks (Jobs):**

- Manage and balance the unit's financial travel budget and their allocated CO₂ emission budget.
- Align the team's operational needs (e.g., client meetings, conferences) with strict corporate sustainability requirements.
- Report the business unit's ecological footprint and reduction progress to senior management.
- Guide team members towards greener travel choices.

**Pains:**

- **Conflicting Goals:** High pressure from upper management to reduce the unit's carbon footprint without negatively impacting business performance or client relationships.
- **Lack of Visibility:** It is currently very difficult to assess the exact environmental impact of team travel and identify the biggest "CO₂ drivers" within the unit.
- **Persuasion Hurdle:** Hard to convince employees to take longer, more sustainable routes (like trains) instead of quick flights without having clear, hard data to justify the decision.

**Gains:**

- **Clear Trade-offs:** An intuitive dashboard that instantly compares travel options (e.g., Cost vs. Time vs. CO₂ emissions), making approvals much easier.
- **Target Tracking:** A simple, visual overview showing how the business unit is performing against corporate CO₂ reduction targets.
- **Data-Driven Leadership:** Easy-to-export visuals that help communicate the "why" behind travel policies to the team and foster a more sustainable mindset.

#### **Use Case Scenario: Business Unit Manager**

- **Context:** Mark is preparing for a meeting where he needs to report on his unit's budget and progress toward the CO₂ reduction targets.
- **The Action:** The Dashboard filters the data specifically for his Unit for the current year.

**The Insight (How the dashboard helps):**

- **Spotting Inefficiencies:** He immediately sees a prominent bar chart indicating that short flights account for 40% of his team's total travel emissions.
- **Comparing Options:** He uses the dashboard's comparison feature to look at the alternative: traveling by train or Car on the same route. The data visualizes that while the train takes roughly 1.5 hours longer door-to-door, the CO₂ emissions are reduced by over 80%, and the financial cost is significantly lower.

**The Outcome:**

Mark exports this visualization from the dashboard and shares it in his next team meeting. Using the clear data, he establishes a new internal guideline: All travel to Frankfurt must now be done by train unless there is an urgent exception. Mark is happy because he successfully reduced his unit's footprint and saved money, without needing to be a data expert to find this insight.

### **Persona 3: Employee - Frequent Business Traveler**

![](../charta-pictures/alex_hauser.png)


- **Name:** Alex Hauser
- **Age:** 55
- **Role:** Senior Sales Consultant
- **Nationality:** Swiss
- **Degree:** Master in international business administration
- **Domain Expertise:** Low in sustainability; High in client management and sales.
- **Data Literacy:** Low (needs simple, clear visuals; no time for complex data exploration).
- **Technical Environment:** Primarily mobile phone while traveling, laptop in hotels or home office.
- **Frequency of Use:** whenever planning a new trip or right after completing one.

Alex Hauser is an experienced sales veteran. He is 55 years old and originally from Liestal, near Basel, but moved years ago with his family to a suburban house near Zurich to be closer to the corporate headquarters. Alex has been working continuously at the company for 20 years, most of that time as a Senior Key Account Manager for major international clients. He is married and the proud father of two children who are both studying at the university of Zurich. 

His main motivation has always been closing deals and nurturing long-term client relationships. Traveling was never a passion for him, but rather necessary to achieve his sales targets and secure his commissions. He is respected in the industry and known for his negotiation skills.

For Alex technology is a pure tool. He uses his smartphone constantly for calls, emails, and WhatsApp communication with clients while he travels. Personal interaction are more important to him than data-driven analytics. He hates complex software that forces him to learn new dashboards just to create a simple visualization. He has shown interest in exploring CO₂ trends over the last few months but feels overwhelmed by the complexity and feels pressured. His main concern is getting to his destination quickly and efficiently, without having to fight through bureaucratic hurdles. He values clear, uncomplicated solutions that save him time, as he sees his value in direct client contact.

He feels that younger sustainability staff, like Laura, do not understand the pressure of sales. He is frustrated that he now has to justify his travel decisions, which he made instinctively for years. The biggest challenge for him is the fear that the new CO₂ guidelines could be in his way of achieving sales targets. He also has a subtle feeling of guilt regarding CO₂ emissions, as his children sometimes criticize him for flying so much.

**User Tasks (Jobs):**

- Plan upcoming business trips to client meetings efficiently.
- Compare travel options (time vs. CO₂ emissions) to comply with company guidelines.
- Track their personal annual travel footprint.
- Justify travel choices to their manager.

**Pains:**

- **Information Overload:** It is frustrating and time-consuming to figure out which travel route is actually the most sustainable. Alex just wants a quick answer without needing to read through complex data or policies.
- **Time Pressure:** "I just need to get to the client quickly. Figuring out the greenest route takes too much time. I choose to fly to save time."
- **Guilt vs. Efficiency:** Wants to look into traveling more sustainably but feels pressured to choose the fastest option (usually flying) to maximize working hours. 

**Gains:**

- **Quick Comparisons:** A very simple visual comparison (e.g., Train vs. Flight for a specific distance) showing the exact CO₂ savings at a glance. 
- **Personal Impact:** A clear, gamified view of their own travel footprint (e.g., "You saved 200kg of CO₂ this month by taking the train!"). 
- **Easy Decision Making:** Feeling confident that they are making the right choice according to company policy without having to read long rulebooks.

#### **Use Case Scenario: Frequent Business Traveler**

**Context:** 

Alex needs to visit a key client in Munich (from the Zurich headquarters). Usually, Alex would just book a quick flight out of habit to save time.

**Action:** 

Before booking, Alex opens the Business Travel Dashboard on their mobile phone and looks at the "Transport Mode Comparison" for short-haul distances (under 400 km).

**The Insight (How the dashboard helps):**

- **Realizing the Impact:** The dashboard shows a striking bar chart comparing the Zurich-Munich route. Alex instantly sees that the flight produces around 120 kg of CO₂, while the train only produces about 15 kg.
- **Time vs. CO₂:** The scatter plot reveals that when factoring in airport security and travel to the city center, the flight only saves about 45 minutes door-to-door compared to the train, but causes 8 times the emissions.

**The Outcome:**

Alex decides to book the train instead of the flight. The visual evidence was so clear and easy to understand that the decision took less than a minute. Alex uses the continuous 3.5-hour train ride to prepare the sales presentation, which wouldn't be possible on a fragmented flight journey, and feels good about actively reducing their personal carbon footprint.


## Situation Assessment
Dataset:

The dataset contains approcimately 25,000 business travel recorde with variables such as:

- `date` - of travel
- `transport_mode` - transport mode (e.g., flight, train, rental car, etc.)
- `km` - travel distance 
- `CO2e` - estimated CO₂ emissions
- `travel_purpose` - reason for the travel

Personnel:

- Jasmin Fankhauser
- Séverine Stadler
- Lia Sifrig
- Anna Kaufmann

Material: 

- Personal laptops

software/tools:

- Python for data exploration
- Quarto for visualization / dashboard development
- Gemini for creating pictures of personas

Constraints and risks:

- Limited time for data exploration and visualization development
- Possible missing or incomplete data in the dataset
- Learning new tool for dashboard development (Quarto) takes time

## Visualization Concept
The project will create a simple and focused interactive dashboard that helps users understand business travel and emissions.

**Product form**: 

We will create an interactive dashboard that allows users to explore business travel data and CO₂ emissions. The dashboard is designed to help users understand travel purposes, transport modes, and emission patterns.

**Visual encodings**: 

- A line chart showing CO₂ emissions over time (`date`, `CO2e`) This helps users see trends in business travel.
- A bar chart will show emissions by travel purpose (`travel_purpose`, `CO2e`). This helps users understand which types of trips contribute most to emissions.
- A bar chart will show emissions by transport mode (`transport_mode`, `CO2e`). This allows users to compare the environmental impact of different travel options.
- A scatter plot will compare travel duration and CO₂ emissions for individual trips. Each point represents one trip and is colored by transport mode.

Tooltips will show additional details such as travel purpose and transport mode.


**Interactivity**:

The dashboard will include simple interactive elements such as filters for year, transport mode, and travel purpose. Users will also be able to see additional information through tooltips.
These interactive features help users focus on specific parts of the data and compare travel options more easily.

**Narrative and annotation**: 

The dashboard will include clear titles, labels, and short text explanations to help users understand the visualizations. Each chart will have a descriptive title and simple labels so that the meaning of the data is easy to interpret.

Short annotations may be used to highlight important patterns, such as high emission transport modes or noticeable trends over time. These elements guide users through the dashboard and make it easier to understand the key insights without needing detailed background knowledge.

**Target medium and integration**: 

The dashboard will be presented as a web-based prototype, using Quarto, and can be viewed in a browser on a laptop or desktop computer. It is mainly intended for use within the course and for presentation purposes. In a realworld scenario, a similar dashboard could be integrated into a company intranet or reporting system to support internal analysis.

**Cognitive and analytical value**: 

The dashboard helps users understand patterns in travel data.
Line charts should show trends over time and show how emissions develop also during events such as the COVID-19 pandemic. The Bar charts allow users to compare emissions across different travel purposes and transport modes, making it easier to identify which options have the biggest impact on emissions and could be improved to reduce their environmental footprint. The scatter plot helps users see the relationship between travel duration and emissions for individual trips.

**Communicative value**: 

The dashboard presents key information clearly without interpreting or evaluating the results. It is designed as an exploratory tool that allows users to analyse the data and draw their own conclusions.

**Experiential and aesthetic value**: 

The dashboard is designed to be simple and easy to use. The layout is clear and not overloaded, so users can quickly understand what they see.
Interactive elements like filters and tooltips allow users to explore the data step by step and focus on what is important for them. This makes the dashboard more interesting to use.
The clear structure helps users trust the data and understand how the charts are connected. Because the dashboard is easy to use and shows useful information, users are more likely to use it in practice.

## Project Plan Séverine Stadler
Divide the project into individual phases, describe them briefly and draw up a preliminary timetable, e.g. as a Gantt chart:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-project-plan
%%| fig-cap: Preliminary project plan in the form of a Gantt chart.
gantt
    title Project Plan
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    tickInterval 5day
    section Project Understanding
        Initiation workshop and context analysis     :a1, 2024-07-01, 1d
        Stakeholder and user analysis     :a3, 2024-07-02, 5d
        Situation assessment    :a4, 2024-07-06, 1d
        Project objectives and visualization concept    :a5, 2024-07-07, 1d
        Sign project charta: milestone, m1, 2024-07-08, 1d
    
    section Data Acquisition and Exploration
        Acquire data :a6, 2024-07-08, 3d
        Exploratory data analysis   :a7, 2024-07-09, 2d
        Discuss data report: milestone, m2, 2024-07-11, 1d
        
    section Visual Encoding and Design
        Overview charts   :a8, 2024-07-11, 3d
        Maps :a9, 2024-07-11, 5d
        Implement Dashboard prototype :a10, 2024-07-16, 5d
        Complete visualization report :a10, 2024-07-21, 1d
    section Evaluation
        Prepare presentation :a10, 2024-07-22, 2d
        Project presentation : milestone, m2, 2024-07-24, 4m
```

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-project-plan
%%| fig-cap: Preliminary project plan in the form of a Gantt chart based on the 2026 semester schedule.
gantt
    title Project Plan (Based on Semester Schedule)
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    tickInterval 1week
    axisFormat %Y-%m-%d

    section Project Understanding
        Initiation workshop and context analysis     :a1, 2026-02-19, 2d
        Stakeholder and user analysis (Personas)    :a3, 2026-02-21, 6d
        Situation assessment                        :a4, 2026-02-27, 2d
        Project objectives and visualization concept :a5, 2026-03-01, 4d
        Sign project charta: milestone, m1, 2026-03-05, 1d
    
    section Data Acquisition and Exploration
        Acquire data                                :a6, 2026-03-06, 5d
        Exploratory data analysis                   :a7, 2026-03-11, 7d
        Discuss data report: milestone, m2, 2026-03-19, 1d
        
    section Visual Encoding and Design
        Overview charts (Feedback & coaching)       :a8, 2026-03-20, 10d
        Maps (Visualizing Geo Data)                  :a9, 2026-03-31, 10d
        Implement Dashboard prototype                :a10, 2026-04-10, 25d
        Complete visualization report               :a11, 2026-05-05, 5d
    section Evaluation
        Presentation Training                        :a12, 2026-05-15, 6d
        Project presentation : milestone, m3, 2026-05-21, 1d

```

See [Mermaid syntax for Gantt charts](https://mermaid.js.org/syntax/gantt.html). It might not be displayed correctly in Safari &#8594; use Chrome. [Live editor with export functionality](https://mermaid.live/)

## Roles and Contact Details
- Jasmin Fankhauser - Dashboard Visualization and Presentation (fankhjas@students.zhaw.ch)
- Séverine Stadler - Project Charta and Dashboard Visualization (stadlsev@students.zhaw.ch)
- Lia Sifrig - Project Charta and Dashboard Visualization (sifrilia@students.zhaw.ch)
- Anna Kaufmann - Dashboard Visualization and Presentation (kaufma03@students.zhaw.ch)
